title: Best Online Prescription Glasses in Canada: Honest Comparison for 2026
description: Comparing the best places to buy prescription glasses online in Canada — Clearly, Zenni, EyeBuyDirect, and Warby Parker. Pros, cons, and when to buy in-store.
tldr: Clearly is the top online option for Canadians (fast shipping, good return policy, Canadian company). Zenni is cheapest. EyeBuyDirect offers the best variety. All work well for simple single vision prescriptions but are risky for progressives, strong prescriptions, or first-time buyers.

tldrStats.label: Lens type best bought in-store, not online

definition.text: Online prescription glasses are eyeglasses ordered through internet retailers where customers input their prescription details, select frames from digital catalogs, choose lens options, and receive finished glasses by mail. Major Canadian and international online eyewear retailers include Clearly, Zenni Optical, EyeBuyDirect, Warby Parker, and Bonlook.

sections.content:
The process is straightforward: you upload or enter your prescription, browse frames, select lens type and coatings, and the retailer makes and ships your glasses. Most orders arrive within 1-3 weeks.

You'll need:

  • A current prescription (within 2 years in most provinces)
  • Your pupillary distance (PD) — the distance between your pupils in millimetres
  • For progressives: fitting height measurements (which is where things get tricky online)

If you don't have your PD, you can ask your optometrist to include it on your prescription, or measure it yourself using online guides (though professional measurement is more accurate). Next time you're at an eye exam, ask your optometrist to write down your PD.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. Their optometrists assess your overall eye health, discuss your visual needs, and recommend lens and frame options tailored to your lifestyle. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

Clearly (clearly.ca) is owned by EssilorLuxottica and operates out of Vancouver. As a Canadian company, they offer the smoothest experience for Canadian buyers.

Pros:

  • Ships from Canada — faster delivery, no customs fees
  • Prices in Canadian dollars
  • Good selection of house brands and some designer frames
  • Virtual try-on tool
  • Solid return/exchange policy
  • Contact lens and glasses in one place

Cons:

  • Mid-range pricing — not the cheapest online option
  • Designer brand selection is more limited than in-store
  • Progressive lens fitting still carries the same risks as any online retailer

Best for: Canadian buyers who want a reliable, mid-range online experience with local shipping and returns.

The best way to evaluate frame styles is to try them on in person. Photos and online try-on tools give a rough idea, but they can't replicate how a frame feels on your face — the weight distribution, the temple pressure, and how the nose bridge sits. These comfort factors matter more than aesthetics for glasses you'll wear 12+ hours a day.

Charm Optical in Edmonton carries over 500 frames from brands including Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, Prada, and Dolce & Gabbana. Their opticians help you narrow down the options based on your face shape, prescription requirements (especially important for progressives), and personal style preferences. With same-day service for many prescriptions, you can walk in and walk out with new glasses the same day.

Zenni (zennioptical.com) is the go-to for rock-bottom prices. Frames start at $6 USD, and a complete pair with basic lenses can cost under $30.

Pros:

  • Unbeatable prices — hundreds of frames under $30
  • Enormous selection (thousands of styles)
  • Decent coating options at low prices
  • Good for backup pairs or fashion frames you won't wear every day

Cons:

  • Ships from China — longer delivery (2-4 weeks to Canada)
  • Prices in USD (factor in exchange rate)
  • Lens quality is basic at the lowest price points
  • Frame quality is hit-or-miss at the cheapest tier
  • Returns are limited — exchanges only, no refunds on prescription orders

Best for: Budget buyers who want a second or third pair, simple single vision prescriptions, or people who like to experiment with different styles cheaply.

EyeBuyDirect (eyebuydirect.com) splits the difference between Zenni's rock-bottom prices and Clearly's quality.

Pros:

  • Wide frame variety with interesting designs
  • Frequent sales (BOGO, percentage off)
  • Decent lens upgrade options
  • Better frame quality than Zenni at comparable prices

Cons:

  • Ships from China — same delivery timeline as Zenni
  • Prices in USD
  • Progressive lenses have the same online fitting limitations

Best for: Buyers who want more style variety than Zenni with slightly better quality, and are okay waiting for international shipping.

With same-day service for many prescriptions, you can walk in and walk out with new glasses the same day.

Warby Parker (warbyparker.com) is the premium online option with an emphasis on style and social responsibility.

Pros:

  • Stylish, well-designed house brand frames
  • Home try-on program (they ship you 5 frames to test)
  • Good lens quality
  • Some physical retail locations (mostly US, limited Canada)

Cons:

  • Higher prices than other online retailers ($130+ per frame)
  • Limited frame variety compared to Zenni/EyeBuyDirect
  • Mostly house brands — no third-party designers
  • Canadian availability has been inconsistent

Best for: Style-conscious buyers who want a curated selection and are willing to pay more for a polished experience.

sections.h2: When to Skip Online and Buy In-Store
sections.content:
Online glasses work great in some situations and poorly in others. Buy in-store when:

  • You need progressive lenses. Fitting height measurement is critical for progressives, and getting it wrong means distorted vision, headaches, and a difficult adaptation. Professional in-person measurement is the only reliable way.
  • Your prescription is strong (SPH beyond +/-4.00). High prescriptions benefit from careful frame selection (size, curvature) and high-index lens recommendations that are hard to get right remotely.
  • You've never worn glasses before. First-time wearers benefit from professional guidance on frame fit, lens options, and what to expect.
  • You have prism in your prescription. Prism is complex and needs precise fitting.
  • You want premium lens quality. In-store optical shops typically offer higher-tier lenses from manufacturers like Essilor, Zeiss, and Hoya that aren't available through most online retailers.

For any of these situations, visiting a store like Charm Optical in South Edmonton gives you access to professional fitting, premium lens options, and the ability to try frames on before committing. Direct insurance billing is available for most Alberta plans.

sections.h2: Tips for a Successful Online Glasses Purchase
sections.content:
If you decide to buy online, set yourself up for success:

  • Get a professional PD measurement. DIY PD methods are approximate. A 2mm error can cause eye strain and headaches.
  • Stick to single vision. The fitting risks are lowest with single vision lenses.
  • Don't go cheapest possible on lenses. Upgrade to at least mid-tier anti-reflective coating — the cheapest coatings degrade quickly.
  • Order from a retailer with a good return policy. Clearly and Warby Parker have the best policies for Canadian buyers.
  • Keep your old glasses. If the new pair doesn't work out, you need a backup while you sort out returns or remakes.
  • Get an adjustment in-store. Even online glasses need temple and nose pad adjustments. Many local opticians will adjust online-purchased glasses for a small fee or free.

sections.h2: Insurance Coverage for Glasses in Canada
sections.content:
Most Canadians with employer-sponsored health benefits have optical coverage that helps pay for glasses. Typical plans cover $200-$500 every 1-2 years toward frames and lenses combined. Some plans separate frame and lens allowances, while others combine them into a single optical benefit. The specifics depend on your employer's plan — always check your benefit booklet or call your insurer before shopping.

In Alberta, common insurance providers for optical coverage include Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, Desjardins, and Great-West Life. Government programs like AISH and Alberta Works also provide optical benefits for eligible residents. Children under 18 in Alberta can access basic optical coverage through the Alberta Child Health Benefit.

The easiest way to use your benefits is through direct billing — the optical store submits the claim to your insurer electronically, and you only pay the remaining balance at the counter. Charm Optical in Edmonton offers direct insurance billing for most Alberta insurance providers, including Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins. This means no out-of-pocket costs up front and no paperwork to submit later. book an eye exam online to get your prescription updated and use your benefits before they reset.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter.

Whether you are buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: Are online prescription glasses as good as store-bought?
faqs.a: For simple single vision prescriptions, online glasses can be perfectly fine. The lens quality at budget price points is lower, but mid-range online options are comparable. For progressive lenses and complex prescriptions, in-store fitting makes a significant quality difference.

faqs.q: What's the cheapest place to buy glasses online in Canada?
faqs.a: Zenni Optical has the lowest prices, with complete pairs starting under $30 CAD. Clearly is the cheapest Canadian-based option. Keep in mind that the cheapest options often come with basic lens quality and limited return policies.

faqs.q: Can I use my Canadian insurance for online glasses?
faqs.a: Most insurance plans reimburse online glasses purchases, but you'll need to submit the receipt manually rather than getting direct billing. Some plans may have restrictions. In-store purchases at places like Charm Optical offer direct billing, which is simpler.

faqs.q: Should I buy progressive lenses online?
faqs.a: It's generally not recommended. Progressive lenses require precise fitting height measurements that are difficult to take accurately at home. An error of even a couple of millimetres can cause headaches, dizziness, and poor vision. Get progressives fitted in person at an optical store.

faqs.q: Does insurance cover glasses in Canada?
faqs.a: Most employer health plans in Canada include optical coverage of $200-$500 every 1-2 years. Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins are common providers. Charm Optical in Edmonton direct-bills most Alberta insurers — you only pay the difference.

faqs.q: Where is the best place to buy glasses in Edmonton?
faqs.a: Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton carries 500+ designer frames, offers same-day glasses, direct insurance billing, and professional fitting. They carry Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, and more.
